tier_2_handbook.definition: Endometrial cancer (also known as adenocarcinoma of the endometrium, or more broadly, uterine cancer or carcinoma of the uterine corpus) is a malignant epithelial tumor arising from the endometrial lining of the uterus, the innermost layer of the myometrium. The uterus is a hollow, muscular organ of the female reproductive system, embryologically derived from the Müllerian ducts (paramesonephric ducts). The endometrium is the glandular and stromal tissue lining the uterine cavity, which undergoes cyclic changes under hormonal influence during the menstrual cycle. Anatomically, the uterus is situated in the pelvis, posterior to the bladder and anterior to the rectum. It consists of the fundus (superior portion), the corpus (body), and the cervix (inferior portion). The endometrium can be divided functionally into two layers: the stratum functionalis, which is shed during menstruation, and the deeper basalis, which remains and regenerates the functional layer. Uterine sarcomas are a distinct group of uncommon malignant mesenchymal tumors arising from the connective tissue, smooth muscle, or other non-epithelial components of the uterine wall. They account for approximately 3% of all uterine cancers. The myometrium is the thick middle layer of smooth muscle, and the serosa is the outer peritoneal covering. Sarcomas can originate from the myometrial smooth muscle (leiomyosarcoma), endometrial stromal cells (endometrial stromal sarcoma), or other mesenchymal elements. The NCCN Guidelines encompass both malignant epithelial tumors (carcinomas) and uterine sarcomas, as they require different management strategies (see UN-1, Initial Evaluation). [References: UN-1, Discussion MS-3, UTSARC-A 1]

subtypes.name: Endometrioid Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: The most common histologic type of endometrial carcinoma, resembling the normal endometrial glands. It is typically estrogen-dependent and often presents at an early stage with favorable prognosis. Graded from G1 (well-differentiated) to G3 (poorly differentiated).
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 80% of endometrial cancers are endometrioid.
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with unopposed estrogen exposure, obesity, diabetes, hypertension, and Lynch syndrome. Molecular subtypes include POLE-mutated, MSI-H/dMMR, NSMP, and p53 aberrant. Early symptoms include metrorrhagia or postmenopausal vaginal bleeding.

subtypes.name: Serous Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A high-grade, aggressive non-endometrioid histologic subtype. It typically arises in older women and has a propensity for early extrauterine spread, even with apparently early-stage disease.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for about 10% of endometrial cancers.
subtypes.key_associations: Highly aggressive behavior, high risk of metastasis. Strongly associated with p53 aberrant molecular profile. Often presents with advanced stage disease. Associated with older age, nulliparity, and less commonly with obesity compared to endometrioid type.

subtypes.name: Clear Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A non-endometrioid histologic subtype characterized by cells with clear cytoplasm and hobnail or papillary patterns. It is considered a high-risk histology.
subtypes.frequency: Less common, comprising about 5% of endometrial carcinomas.
subtypes.key_associations: Aggressive behavior with higher risk of recurrence and metastasis. Associated with p53 aberrant molecular profile. Often presents at an advanced stage.

subtypes.name: Carcinosarcoma
subtypes.description: Also known as malignant mixed Müllerian tumor (MMMT). This is a biphasic tumor containing both malignant epithelial (carcinoma) and malignant mesenchymal (sarcoma) components. Per NCCN guidelines, carcinosarcomas are now considered and treated as high-grade endometrial cancers (carcinomas), not uterine sarcomas.
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for approximately 3-5% of uterine cancers.
subtypes.key_associations: Highly aggressive with a poor prognosis. Both homologous (elements indigenous to the uterus) and heterologous (elements not native to the uterus, e.g., skeletal muscle, cartilage) stromal elements can be present. Associated with HER2 overexpression in a subset. Often presents at an advanced stage.

subtypes.name: Undifferentiated/Dedifferentiated Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Undifferentiated carcinoma shows a lack of recognizable differentiation toward any specific cell line. Dedifferentiated carcinoma consists of a juxtaposition of undifferentiated carcinoma with a well-differentiated endometrioid component.
subtypes.key_associations: High-grade, aggressive histology with poor prognosis. Often presents at an advanced stage.

subtypes.name: Low-Grade Endometrial Stromal Sarcoma (ESS)
subtypes.description: A mesenchymal tumor composed of cells resembling the endometrial stroma of the proliferative phase. It is characterized by infiltrative growth with tongue-like projections into the myometrium and frequent lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI).
subtypes.frequency: The most common uterine sarcoma subtype after leiomyosarcoma, accounting for about 21% of uterine sarcomas.
subtypes.key_associations: Indolent but can recur or metastasize years after diagnosis. Associated with JAZF1, PHF1, or EPC1 gene fusions. Often expresses estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR), making it amenable to endocrine therapy.

subtypes.name: High-Grade Endometrial Stromal Sarcoma
subtypes.description: An aggressive ESS variant with distinct genetic rearrangements. Subtypes include YWHAE::NUTM2A/B fusion-positive and BCOR-altered (ZC3H7B::BCOR fusion or BCOR internal tandem duplication).
subtypes.key_associations: More aggressive clinical behavior than low-grade ESS. YWHAE-altered tumors have a high-grade round cell component. BCOR-altered tumors have high-grade spindle cells in a myxoid matrix. Shows different molecular features and requires differentiation from other high-grade sarcomas.

subtypes.name: Uterine Leiomyosarcoma (uLMS)
subtypes.description: The most common uterine sarcoma subtype, originating from the smooth muscle of the myometrium. It can be of conventional (spindle cell), epithelioid, or myxoid histologic type.
subtypes.frequency: The most common subtype, accounting for about 63% of uterine sarcomas.
subtypes.key_associations: Highly aggressive with a high rate of hematogenous metastasis (especially to the lungs) and recurrence. Complex karyotypes are hallmark; commonly altered genes include TP53, ATRX, RB1, and PTEN. Prognosis is best predicted by stage.

subtypes.name: Müllerian Adenosarcoma
subtypes.description: A biphasic tumor with a benign, often metaplastic, epithelial component and an atypical, usually low-grade, mesenchymal component. Sarcomatous overgrowth (SO), defined as sarcoma comprising ≥25% of the tumor volume, indicates a worse prognosis.
subtypes.key_associations: Generally indolent, but presence of SO or high-grade cytologic features confers a worse prognosis. May harbor BAP1 loss, ESR1 fusions, or other alterations.

subtypes.name: Perivascular Epithelioid Cell Tumor (PEComa)
subtypes.description: A mesenchymal neoplasm composed of perivascular epithelioid and/or spindled cells that co-express melanocytic and muscle markers.
subtypes.key_associations: Behavior is predicted by tumor stratification into uncertain malignant potential or malignant based on features like size, nuclear grade, mitotic activity, necrosis, and invasion. Associated with inactivating mutations of TSC1/TSC2 and TFE3 fusions. Treatment with mTOR inhibitors may be considered for advanced disease.

subtypes.name: Inflammatory Myofibroblastic Tumor (IMT)
subtypes.description: A spindle cell neoplasm with admixed inflammatory infiltrate. Histologic patterns vary from myxoid hypocellular areas to more compact cellular areas.
subtypes.key_associations: Typically benign and confined to the uterus, but recurrence and extrauterine spread can occur. Often harbors ALK rearrangements. May respond to ALK inhibitors.

subtypes.name: Rhabdomyosarcoma (RMS)
subtypes.description: An aggressive, heterogeneous group of tumors with skeletal muscle differentiation. Subtypes include embryonal, alveolar, and pleomorphic.
subtypes.key_associations: Embryonal subtype has the best prognosis. Molecular alterations differ: DICER1 mutations in embryonal, FOXO1 fusions in alveolar. Expresses myogenic biomarkers (myogenin, MyoD1).

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Endometrial carcinoma exhibits distinct molecular pathogenesis. The TCGA classification identifies four clinically significant molecular subgroups: 1) POLE-mutated: Characterized by mutations in the exonuclease domain of DNA polymerase epsilon (POLE), often high-grade but with excellent prognosis. Hotspot mutations include P286R and V411L. 2) Microsatellite Instability-High (MSI-H) / Mismatch Repair Deficient (dMMR): Results from defective DNA mismatch repair, often due to MLH1 promoter methylation or germline mutations (Lynch syndrome). Associated with intermediate prognosis and potential sensitivity to immune checkpoint inhibitors. 3) No Specific Molecular Profile (NSMP): Corresponds to copy-number-low/wild-type p53 tumors. Typically endometrioid histology, estrogen-dependent, with favorable prognosis. 4) p53 Aberrant: Characterized by TP53 mutations, associated with serous histology, high copy-number alterations, and the worst prognosis. Requires multimodality treatment. Molecular testing for POLE mutations, MMR/MSI status, and p53 IHC is recommended to complement morphologic assessment (ENDO-A). For uterine sarcomas, molecular profiling is key for classification: - Low-grade ESS: Associated with JAZF1::SUZ12, JAZF1::PHF1, EPC1::PHF1 fusions. - High-grade ESS: YWHAE::NUTM2 fusions, ZC3H7B::BCOR fusions, or BCOR internal tandem duplication. - Leiomyosarcoma: Complex karyotypes; commonly altered genes include TP53, ATRX, RB1, PTEN. - PEComa: TSC1/TSC2 mutations, TFE3 fusions. - IMT: ALK rearrangements. - RMS: DICER1 mutations (embryonal), FOXO1 fusions (alveolar). Comprehensive genomic profiling is encouraged in metastatic disease to identify targets like NTRK, RET, and TMB (UTSARC-A). [References: ENDO-A, UTSARC-A, Discussion MS-4, MS-32-34]

epidemiology.incidence: Adenocarcinoma of the endometrium is the fourth most common malignancy of the female genital tract in the United States. It is estimated that 69,120 new uterine cancer cases will occur in 2025. The incidence of uterine corpus cancer has continued to increase by about 1% per year since the mid-2000s.
epidemiology.mortality: It is estimated that 13,860 deaths will result from uterine cancer in 2025. It is noted as the only cancer with reduced survival over the past four decades.
epidemiology.trends: The incidence is increasing, attributed to increased life expectancy, obesity, and a higher rate of advanced-stage cancers and high-risk histologies at diagnosis.
epidemiology.demographics: The typical age group at risk is ≥55 years. Survival is increased in patients who are younger, have early-stage disease, and have lower-grade disease. Racial and ethnic disparities in access to minimally invasive surgical care have been observed. Uterine sarcomas are uncommon, accounting for approximately 3% of all uterine cancers. Among sarcomas, leiomyosarcoma is the most common subtype (63%), followed by endometrial stromal sarcoma (21%). [References: Discussion MS-1, MS-3]

risk_factors.detail: Increased levels of endogenous or exogenous estrogen without opposing progesterone is a primary risk factor. This includes obesity (peripheral conversion in adipose tissue), chronic anovulation, polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS), and exogenous estrogen therapy without progestin.
A major risk factor. Fat cells convert androgens to estrogens via aromatase. The incidence of endometrial cancer increases with body mass index (BMI).
An independent risk factor, likely related to hyperinsulinemia, which may promote endometrial proliferation.
Often associated with obesity and diabetes, and is an independent risk factor.

Hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC) syndrome, caused by germline mutations in mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2). Carriers have a lifetime risk of endometrial cancer of up to 60%. Endometrial cancer may be the first manifestation.

Selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) used for breast cancer treatment and prevention. It has agonist effects on the endometrium, increasing the risk of endometrial hyperplasia and cancer with prolonged use (typically >2 years).
Never having been pregnant. Pregnancy has a protective effect due to progestin dominance.
Prolonged exposure to cyclic estrogen without the protective effect of progesterone from ovulation.
The risk increases with age, with most cases diagnosed after age 55.
Atypical endometrial hyperplasia is a direct precursor lesion for endometrioid adenocarcinoma.
A family history of endometrial, ovarian, or colorectal cancer, even in the absence of known Lynch syndrome, may increase risk.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: The classic presentation of endometrial carcinoma is abnormal uterine bleeding, most commonly postmenopausal bleeding (vaginal bleeding after menopause), which prompts investigation. In premenopausal women, it may manifest as menorrhagia or intermenstrual bleeding. Patients may also present with pelvic pain or pressure, though this is less common. For uterine sarcomas, presentation often includes a rapidly enlarging pelvic mass, which may be painful, or abnormal bleeding. Adenosarcomas may present as a polypoid mass protruding through the cervix.

symptoms.name: Abnormal uterine bleeding
symptoms.detail: The most common symptom, occurring in about 90% of patients. This includes postmenopausal bleeding, metrorrhagia, or menorrhagia.
symptoms.alarm: Yes, postmenopausal bleeding is an alarm symptom requiring urgent evaluation.

symptoms.name: Pelvic pain or pressure
symptoms.detail: May be present due to tumor size or local spread.

symptoms.name: Pelvic mass
symptoms.detail: More common in sarcomas, which may present as a rapidly growing mass.
symptoms.alarm: Yes, a new or enlarging pelvic mass is concerning.

signs.name: Uterine enlargement
signs.detail: On pelvic examination, the uterus may be palpably enlarged or irregular, especially with larger tumors or sarcomas.

signs.name: Cervical mass or bleeding
signs.detail: With cervical involvement, a friable mass may be seen or palpated.
signs.frequency: Less common, seen in advanced local disease

signs.name: Adnexal mass
signs.detail: May indicate ovarian or tubal involvement, or metastatic disease.

atypical_or_missed: Symptoms attributed to menopause or other benign conditions leading to delayed diagnosis
Presentation with metastatic symptoms (e.g., cough from lung metastases, bone pain) without prior uterine symptoms
Tumor incidental finding in a specimen removed for benign indications (e.g., myomectomy)

red_flags: Premenopausal abnormal uterine bleeding not responsive to medical management
Pelvic pain with a palpable adnexal or uterine mass
Abnormal cervical cytology suggesting a high-grade lesion
Ascites or evidence of peritoneal disease
Symptoms suggestive of metastatic disease (e.g., hemoptysis, bone pain, neurological deficits)

diagnostic.test: Endometrial biopsy or dilation and curettage (D&C)
diagnostic.rationale: Direct sampling of the endometrium to obtain histologic diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: Confirmation of adenocarcinoma (type, grade) or other histology (sarcoma).

diagnostic.rationale: Direct visualization of the uterine cavity and targeted biopsy of lesions.
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of focal lesions, polyps, or diffuse disease.

diagnostic.rationale: Crucial for accurate histologic subtyping and grading, especially for high-risk or unusual histologies.
diagnostic.key_findings: Distinction between endometrioid, serous, clear cell, carcinosarcoma, undifferentiated carcinoma, and sarcomas.

staging.rationale: Best imaging for local staging: depth of myometrial invasion, cervical stromal involvement, and local extent.
staging.indication: Standard for planning fertility-sparing treatment and for high-grade or advanced disease.

staging.rationale: Evaluates for metastatic disease, particularly in high-grade histologies or advanced stage.
staging.indication: Recommended for high-grade carcinomas (serous, clear cell, carcinosarcoma, undifferentiated) and when extrauterine disease is suspected.

staging.rationale: May help in evaluating equivocal findings on other imaging or to assess for metastatic disease.
staging.indication: Consider in select patients with suspected metastases.
Can be considered as initial imaging; if abnormal, proceed to chest CT.

staging.rationale: May be used if uterine size is unclear on exam.

biomarkers.test: Immunohistochemistry (IHC) for mismatch repair (MMR) proteins (MLH1, PMS2, MSH2, MSH6) or microsatellite instability (MSI) testing
biomarkers.rationale: Identifies dMMR/MSI-H tumors, which have prognostic implications and predict response to immunotherapy.
biomarkers.when: Recommended at diagnosis for all endometrial carcinomas.
biomarkers.mandatory: Strongly recommended to complement morphologic assessment.

biomarkers.rationale: Identifies POLE-mutated tumors, which are often high-grade but have an excellent prognosis.
biomarkers.when: Recommended at diagnosis as part of molecular classification.

biomarkers.rationale: Surrogate for TP53 mutation, identifying the most aggressive molecular subtype (p53 aberrant).
biomarkers.when: Recommended for all endometrial carcinomas, especially high-grade ones.

biomarkers.test: HER2 IHC (with reflex to FISH if equivocal)
biomarkers.rationale: Required for p53-aberrant carcinomas (e.g., serous) and all metastatic/recurrent disease to guide trastuzumab-based therapy.
biomarkers.when: For p53-aberrant tumors and all metastatic/recurrent disease.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes for specified histologies and stages.

biomarkers.rationale: Identifies tumors with NTRK fusions, which respond to TRK inhibitors.
biomarkers.when: Consider for metastatic or recurrent endometrial carcinoma.

biomarkers.rationale: Identifies tumors with RET fusions, which respond to RET inhibitors.
biomarkers.when: Consider for metastatic or recurrent endometrial carcinoma.

biomarkers.rationale: High TMB (≥10 mut/Mb) identifies tumors likely to respond to pembrolizumab.
biomarkers.when: Consider for recurrent or metastatic disease.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but recommended if not previously done.

biomarkers.test: Estrogen receptor (ER) and progesterone receptor (PR) testing
biomarkers.rationale: May guide hormonal therapy options in recurrent/metastatic disease.
biomarkers.when: Recommended in stage III, IV, and recurrent disease.
biomarkers.mandatory: Yes for advanced and recurrent disease.

do_not_miss: Molecular testing (MMR/MSI, POLE, p53) for all endometrial carcinomas
Expert pathology review for accurate histologic subtyping
Pelvic MRI for fertility-sparing treatment planning
avoid: Routine whole abdominal RT as adjuvant therapy (ineffective and toxic)
Vaginal cytology for surveillance (low yield in asymptomatic patients)
Intraperitoneal morcellation of uterine specimens (increases risk of spillage)

staging.system: All staging in this guideline is based on the 2009 FIGO surgical staging system and the AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th Edition.

t_stages.description: Tumor confined to the corpus uteri, including endocervical glandular involvement.
Tumor limited to the endometrium or invading less than half the myometrium.
Tumor invading one half or more of the myometrium.
Tumor invading the stromal connective tissue of the cervix but not extending beyond the uterus. Does NOT include endocervical glandular involvement.
Tumor involving serosa, adnexa, vagina, or parametrium.
Tumor involving the serosa and/or adnexa (direct extension or metastasis).
Vaginal involvement (direct extension or metastasis) or parametrial involvement.
Tumor invading the bladder mucosa and/or bowel mucosa (bullous edema is not sufficient to classify a tumor as T4).

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Isolated tumor cells in regional lymph node(s) no greater than 0.2 mm.
Regional lymph node metastasis to pelvic lymph nodes.
Regional lymph node metastasis (greater than 0.2 mm but not greater than 2.0 mm in diameter) to pelvic lymph nodes.
Regional lymph node metastasis (greater than 2.0 mm in diameter) to pelvic lymph nodes.
Regional lymph node metastasis to para-aortic lymph nodes, with or without positive pelvic lymph nodes.
Regional lymph node metastasis (greater than 0.2 mm but not greater than 2.0 mm in diameter) to para-aortic lymph nodes, with or without positive pelvic lymph nodes.
Regional lymph node metastasis (greater than 2.0 mm in diameter) to para-aortic lymph nodes, with or without positive pelvic lymph nodes.

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis (includes metastasis to inguinal lymph nodes, intraperitoneal disease, lung, liver, or bone). It excludes metastasis to pelvic or para-aortic lymph nodes, vagina, uterine serosa, or adnexa.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor confined to the corpus uteri.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Not explicitly stated in the source text for this group alone; varies by histology and grade.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor limited to the endometrium or invading less than half the myometrium.
Tumor invading one half or more of the myometrium.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Varies by grade; worse for high-grade histologies.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor invading cervical stroma but not beyond uterus.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Intermediate; influenced by grade and other factors.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor involving serosa, adnexa, vagina, or parametrium.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Poor for high-grade histologies; better for low-grade endometrioid.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional lymph node metastasis to pelvic lymph nodes.
Regional lymph node metastasis to para-aortic lymph nodes, with or without positive pelvic nodes.
Tumor invading bladder and/or bowel mucosa.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative with multimodality therapy, if resectable.

staging_pearls: Positive peritoneal cytology does not affect the FIGO stage but is an adverse risk factor.
Isolated tumor cells (ITCs) in lymph nodes are staged N0(i+) and do not upstage patients, but may influence adjuvant therapy discussions.
The presence of sarcomatous overgrowth (SO) in adenosarcoma is a poor prognostic factor and influences stage-grouping for sarcoma staging.
Molecular subtype (POLE-mutated, MSI-H, p53-aberrant, NSMP) provides additional prognostic information beyond anatomic stage.
For uterine sarcomas, a separate staging system (AJCC 8th Edition) is used, which is based on tumor size and extent rather than depth of invasion.

management_principles.overview: The management of uterine neoplasms is based on a multidisciplinary approach, integrating surgery, radiation therapy (RT), systemic therapy, and molecular profiling. Treatment intent varies from curative for localized disease to palliative for advanced or recurrent disease. The guidelines emphasize the importance of expert pathology review, molecular analysis (including POLE mutations, MMR/MSI, p53, HER2, ER/PR, NTRK, RET, and TMB testing), and individualized treatment based on histologic type, stage, and risk factors. Surgical staging is recommended for most patients, with minimally invasive surgery preferred when feasible. Adjuvant therapy decisions are guided by pathologic findings, and molecular subtypes may influence therapy escalation or de-escalation. Clinical trial enrollment is strongly encouraged.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with localized or regionally confined disease suitable for surgery with or without adjuvant therapy.
by_intent.approach: Primary treatment typically involves surgical resection (TH/BSO with staging) followed by risk-adapted adjuvant therapy (RT, systemic therapy, or observation). For medically inoperable patients, definitive RT with or without brachytherapy is an alternative. Fertility-sparing options are considered for select patients with low-risk disease.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with advanced, recurrent, or metastatic disease not amenable to curative surgery.
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, or hormonal therapy) is the mainstay, often combined with RT for symptom control. Local therapies like SBRT or ablation may be used for oligometastatic disease. Best supportive care is emphasized for patients with poor performance status.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Multidisciplinary expertise is recommended for all patients, involving gynecologic oncologists for surgical management, pathologists for histologic and molecular analysis, radiologists for imaging, radiation oncologists for RT planning, and medical oncologists for systemic therapy. For uterine sarcomas, referral to a center specializing in mesenchymal tumors is advised.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is considered in surgical eligibility and treatment intensity. Patients with good performance status (e.g., ECOG 0-1) are candidates for aggressive multimodality therapy, while those with poor performance status may receive reduced-intensity or palliative approaches. Detailed guidelines are referenced in the NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology for age-related considerations.

management_pathways.title: Endometrial Carcinoma with Disease Limited to the Uterus (Pure Endometrioid Histology)

nodes.criteria: Medically operable patients with clinically uterine-confined disease.

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging
options.recommendation: Minimally invasive surgery is preferred when feasible. Includes peritoneal lavage, lymph node assessment (SLN mapping preferred), and omental biopsy for high-risk histologies.

options.label: Adjuvant RT based on stage and risk factors
options.recommendation: For Stage IA G1-2: observation or vaginal brachytherapy if LVSI or age ≥60y. For Stage IA G3: vaginal brachytherapy preferred. For Stage IB: vaginal brachytherapy preferred, with consideration of EBRT for higher risk factors. For Stage II: EBRT preferred with or without brachytherapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy is based on surgical-pathologic findings: Stage I (observation or vaginal brachytherapy), Stage II (EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy), Stages III-IV (systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy). Molecular testing may influence decisions, e.g., POLE-mutated tumors may de-escalate therapy.

options.label: Definitive RT with or without brachytherapy
options.recommendation: EBRT to pelvis with or without para-aortic RT, combined with brachytherapy. For uterine-limited disease, brachytherapy alone may be considered for selected cases.

options.label: Hormonal therapy for select patients
options.recommendation: Progestin-based therapy (e.g., levonorgestrel IUD, megestrol, medroxyprogesterone) for low-grade endometrioid histology with ER/PR positivity.

adjuvant_logic.summary: N/A - primary definitive treatment.

management_pathways.title: Endometrial Carcinoma with Suspected or Gross Cervical Involvement

options.label: TH or radical hysterectomy with BSO and surgical staging
options.recommendation: Radical hysterectomy may be performed if needed for negative margins.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treat as disease limited to uterus with adjuvant therapy based on final staging (ENDO-4, ENDO-5, ENDO-6).

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging
options.recommendation: Alternative: EBRT ± brachytherapy followed by TH/BSO 4–12 weeks post-RT.

options.label: EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Definitive RT with re-evaluation for surgery if rendered operable.

adjuvant_logic.summary: For surgically staged Stage II: EBRT preferred with or without brachytherapy and ± systemic therapy (category 2B).

management_pathways.title: Endometrial Carcinoma with Suspected Extrauterine Disease

nodes.criteria: No evidence of extrauterine disease on imaging.

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging
options.recommendation: Treat as disease limited to uterus.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Based on surgical staging.

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging/debulking
options.recommendation: Goal: no measurable residual disease. Preoperative systemic therapy can be considered.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy for Stage III–IV: systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± EBRT ± SBRT ± TH/BSO
options.recommendation: Consider ablation for 1–5 metastatic lesions if primary cancer controlled.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative intent.

management_pathways.title: Adjuvant Treatment for Surgically Staged Endometrial Carcinoma (Endometrioid Histology)

options.label: Observation or vaginal brachytherapy based on risk factors
options.recommendation: Stage IA G1-2: observation preferred or vaginal brachytherapy if LVSI and/or age ≥60y. Stage IA G3: vaginal brachytherapy preferred or observation if no myoinvasion. Stage IB G1: vaginal brachytherapy preferred or observation if age <60y and no LVSI. Stage IB G2: vaginal brachytherapy preferred or EBRT if age ≥60y and/or LVSI. Stage IB G3: RT ± systemic therapy (category 2B).

adjuvant_logic.summary: EBRT initiation when vaginal cuff healed, preferably ≤12 weeks post-surgery.

options.label: EBRT preferred and/or vaginal brachytherapy ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy is category 2B. Consider additional imaging if not previously done.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adverse risk factors (stromal invasion, grade, LVSI, fundal factors) influence therapy choice.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy
options.recommendation: Preferred regimens: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab (category 1 for stage III-IV, except carcinosarcoma), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Dostarlimab (category 1), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Durvalumab (for dMMR only, category 1), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Trastuzumab (for HER2-positive), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab, Carboplatin/Paclitaxel.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy is foundation; add RT for locoregional control.

management_pathways.title: Management After Simple/Total Hysterectomy and BSO Without Surgical Staging

nodes.criteria: Stage IA, G1-2 with myometrial invasion and no LVSI.

options.label: Observation or adjuvant treatment based on imaging
options.recommendation: If imaging negative, consider observation. If suspicious/positive, refer to surgical staging or adjuvant treatment per stage.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation may be sufficient.

nodes.criteria: Stage IA G3 no LVSI, Stage IB any grade, or any myometrial invasive carcinoma with LVSI.

options.label: Consider surgical restaging or RT
options.recommendation: If imaging negative: consider surgical restaging. If not restaged: EBRT and/or vaginal brachytherapy ± systemic therapy (category 2B).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy based on risk factors if not surgically staged.

options.label: Surgical resection if resectable
options.recommendation: If not suitable for surgery, consider interventional radiologic biopsy and adjuvant treatment per stage.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treat based on disease extent.

management_pathways.title: Fertility-Sparing Options for Endometrioid Endometrial Carcinoma

nodes.criteria: All criteria met: Grade 1 endometrioid adenocarcinoma on hysteroscopy/D&C, disease limited to endometrium on MRI, no metastatic disease, no contraindications.

options.label: Continuous progestin-based therapy
options.recommendation: Levonorgestrel IUD (preferred), megestrol, medroxyprogesterone. Consider hysteroscopic tumor resection. Dual-progestin therapy may be considered.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Monitoring with endometrial evaluation every 3–6 months. Complete response: encourage conception. Persistent disease: consider TH/BSO after childbearing or progression.

options.label: Clinical assessment and imaging
options.recommendation: H&P every 3–6 mo for 2–3 y, then every 6–12 mo up to year 5, then annually. CA-125 if initially elevated or serous histology. Imaging as indicated based on symptoms/exam. Consider survivorship care.

adjuvant_logic.summary: N/A

nodes.criteria: No prior RT to recurrence site or prior vaginal brachytherapy only.

options.label: EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Or surgical exploration + resection ± IORT.

options.label: Surgical resection
options.recommendation: Consider for vaginal/pelvic recurrence confined to vagina or paravaginal soft tissue.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Additional therapy based on surgical pathology.

options.label: Surgical exploration + resection ± IORT ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Or systemic therapy ± palliative EBRT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Individualized based on prior treatment and disease extent.

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging and maximal tumor debulking
options.recommendation: For noninvasive stage IA: observe or systemic therapy ± vaginal brachytherapy or vaginal brachytherapy. For invasive stage IA, IB, II: systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy. For stage III, IV: systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Washings can help determine treatment planning. HER2 testing recommended for advanced/metastatic disease.

options.label: EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Re-evaluate for surgical resection based on response.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Definitive treatment intent.

management_pathways.title: Clear Cell Carcinoma of the Endometrium

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging and maximal tumor debulking
options.recommendation: Similar to serous carcinoma: for noninvasive stage IA: observe or systemic therapy ± vaginal brachytherapy or vaginal brachytherapy. For invasive stage IA, IB, II: systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy. For stage III, IV: systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Washings can help determine treatment planning. Principles of molecular analysis apply.

options.label: EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Re-evaluate for surgical resection based on response.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Definitive treatment intent.

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging and maximal tumor debulking
options.recommendation: Additional treatment: systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Aggressive histology; systemic therapy integral.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± EBRT ± vaginal brachytherapy
options.recommendation: Re-evaluate for surgical resection and/or RT based on response.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative or neoadjuvant intent.

options.label: TH/BSO with surgical staging and maximal tumor debulking
options.recommendation: For stage I: systemic therapy + vaginal brachytherapy. For stage II, III, IV: systemic therapy + vaginal brachytherapy ± EBRT. Consider EBRT if high-grade epithelial components and sarcoma dominant.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Chemotherapy initiation within 3–6 weeks postoperatively; vaginal brachytherapy can interdigitate with chemotherapy starting 6 weeks postoperatively. HER2 testing recommended for advanced/metastatic disease.

nodes.criteria: Not suitable for primary surgery; uterine-confined ± pelvic nodal involvement.

options.label: EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Re-evaluate for surgical resection based on response.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Definitive treatment intent.

nodes.criteria: Not suitable for primary surgery; other metastatic disease.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± EBRT or best supportive care
options.recommendation: Palliative intent.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative care.

management_pathways.title: Uterine Sarcoma Diagnosed After TH/SCH ± BSO

nodes.criteria: Tumor initially fragmented or residual cervix/tube/ovary.

options.label: Consider re-exploration/reresection or completion salpingo-oophorectomy
options.preference: Preferred for low-grade ESS or adenosarcoma
options.recommendation: Individualized based on histology and residual disease. For low-grade ESS or adenosarcoma (ER-positive), consider systemic endocrine therapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Expert pathology review and molecular testing recommended. ER/PR testing for LMS, ESS, adenosarcoma to guide ovarian management.

management_pathways.title: Uterine Sarcoma Diagnosed by Biopsy or Myomectomy

options.label: TH with en bloc resection ± BSO
options.recommendation: Additional surgical resection for intraoperative extrauterine disease. Oophorectomy individualized; favor BSO if ER/PR positive. Morcellation avoided.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy based on histologic subtype and stage (UTSARC-2, UTSARC-3).

options.label: Surgical resection based on symptoms, extent, resectability
options.recommendation: Resection of metastatic focus. If no surgical resection: systemic therapy and/or palliative EBRT ± brachytherapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Individualized based on resectability.

options.label: Systemic therapy and/or palliative EBRT ± brachytherapy
options.recommendation: Clinical trial enrollment strongly recommended.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative intent.

management_pathways.title: Low-Grade Endometrial Stromal Sarcoma (ESS) or Adenosarcoma Without Sarcomatous Overgrowth

options.label: BSO (preferred) or observe if prior BSO
options.recommendation: Oophorectomy individualized for reproductive age; favor BSO if ER/PR positive.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation after BSO.

options.label: BSO ± systemic endocrine therapy ± EBRT
options.recommendation: Anti-estrogen hormone therapy: aromatase inhibitors preferred, fulvestrant, megestrol acetate (category 2B), medroxyprogesterone acetate (category 2B). EBRT palliative for stage IVB.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Endocrine therapy for ER/PR-positive tumors; small tumor volume or indolent growth pace preferred.

options.label: BSO or observe if prior BSO
options.recommendation: Observation post-BSO.

adjuvant_logic.summary: N/A

options.label: BSO ± systemic therapy ± EBRT
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy recommended for residual measurable disease. EBRT palliative for stage IVB.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Aggressive histology; systemic therapy considered.

management_pathways.title: High-Grade ESS, Undifferentiated Uterine Sarcoma, Leiomyosarcoma, and Other Sarcomas (e.g., PEComa)

options.label: Observe or consider observation if completely resected with negative margins
options.recommendation: Observation only option; adjuvant systemic therapy and/or EBRT may be considered for high-risk features.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Limited data; observation standard for completely resected stage I.

options.label: Consider systemic therapy and/or EBRT
options.recommendation: Observation may be considered for completely resected tumors with negative margins.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant therapy individualized based on risk factors.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± EBRT ± palliative EBRT
options.recommendation: Stage IVB: systemic therapy ± palliative EBRT. Biomarker-directed therapy options: NTRK fusion-positive tumors (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib), ALK translocation (crizotinib, ceritinib, brigatinib, lorlatinib, alectinib), RET fusion-positive (selpercatinib), PEComa (albumin-bound sirolimus), TMB-H (pembrolizumab, ipilimumab + nivolumab), BRCA-altered LMS (PARP inhibitors), CDK4 amplification (palbociclib).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Metastatic setting; systemic therapy cornerstone.

options.label: Clinical assessment and imaging
options.recommendation: H&P every 3–4 mo (consider every 6 months for low-grade, early-stage) for 2–3 y, then every 6–12 mo. Imaging: chest/abdomen/pelvis CT every 3–6 mo for first 3 y, then every 6–12 mo for next 2 y, then annually or biannually for additional 5 years depending on histology/grade/stage. Optional abdomen/pelvis MRI and chest CT without contrast. FDG-PET/CT if metastasis suspected.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Patient education on recurrence symptoms, lifestyle, survivorship care.

options.label: Surgical resection or other local ablative therapy
options.recommendation: Consider preoperative or postoperative systemic therapy and/or EBRT/SBRT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Metastasectomy historical standard for oligometastatic disease; choice depends on performance status, patient preference, lesion accessibility.

options.label: Systemic therapy and/or local therapy (EBRT/SBRT or local ablative therapy)
options.recommendation: If response, consider surgery ± EBRT/SBRT.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Individualized based on response.

options.label: Systemic therapy ± palliative EBRT or best supportive care
options.recommendation: Clinical trial enrollment encouraged.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative care.

options.label: Surgical exploration + resection ± IORT
options.recommendation: Consider preoperative EBRT ± systemic therapy. For residual disease: EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy.

options.label: EBRT ± brachytherapy ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Primary RT option.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy may be added.

options.label: Surgical exploration + resection ± IORT ± systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Or systemic therapy or selected reirradiation with EBRT and/or brachytherapy.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Reirradiation only in limited disease for palliation.

items.text: History and physical examination (including pelvic exam).
Complete blood count, liver function tests, renal function tests, chemistry profile.
Consider CA-125 for suspected extrauterine disease or high-risk histologies (serous, clear cell, carcinosarcoma).
Expert pathology review with additional endometrial biopsy as clinically indicated.

Imaging: Consider chest imaging (chest x-ray); if abnormal, chest CT without contrast. Consider pelvis MRI to assess local disease extent. For high-grade carcinoma, consider chest/abdomen/pelvis CT (preferred). Consider FDG-PET/CT if metastasis suspected.
items.recommendation: Category 2A. See Principles of Imaging (ENDO-B, UTSARC-B).

items.text: Recommend molecular evaluation of tumor and evaluation for inherited cancer risk (POLE mutations, MMR/MSI, p53, HER2, ER/PR, NTRK, RET, TMB testing).
items.recommendation: Category 2A. See Principles of Molecular Analysis (ENDO-A, UTSARC-A).

items.text: Germline and/or multigene panel testing for Lynch syndrome and other hereditary syndromes.
Assess for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List.
For uterine sarcomas: Consider referral to center of expertise for malignant mesenchymal tumors.
Histologic type and grade determination (including for adenosarcoma: grade, sarcomatous overgrowth).
Assessment of myometrial invasion (depth in mm/myometrial thickness), cervical stromal involvement, LVSI (substantial LVSI defined as ≥5 involved vessels).

items.recommendation: Category 2A. Cytology does not affect FIGO staging but is an adverse risk factor.

items.text: Lymph node assessment: SLN ultrastaging for detection of low-volume metastasis; isolated tumor cells staged as N0(i+).
items.recommendation: Category 2A. SLN mapping preferred for endometrial carcinoma.

items.text: For uterine sarcomas: Expert gynecologic pathology review recommended; molecular analysis for classification (e.g., JAZF1 fusions for low-grade ESS, YWHAE::NUTM2 for high-grade ESS, PLAG1 for myxoid LMS).
Performance status evaluation for surgical and treatment eligibility.
Assessment of comorbidities and risk factors (e.g., obesity, diabetes, Lynch syndrome history).
Multidisciplinary team consultation: gynecologic oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, radiology.
Fertility expert consultation for fertility-sparing options.
Genetic counseling for suspected germline mutations.

surgery.role: Primary treatment for medically operable patients with uterine neoplasms. Surgical staging provides critical prognostic information to guide adjuvant therapy.
principles: TH/BSO with surgical staging is the standard for apparent uterine-confined disease unless fertility-sparing is desired.
Minimally invasive surgery (laparoscopic, robotic) is preferred when technically feasible due to lower morbidity without compromise in oncologic outcome.
Endometrial carcinoma should be removed en bloc; intraperitoneal morcellation or fragmentation should be avoided.
Lymph node assessment includes SLN mapping (preferred) or pelvic ± para-aortic lymphadenectomy. SLN algorithm should be adhered to for accurate staging.
For stage II disease, radical hysterectomy should only be performed if needed to obtain negative margins.

Ovarian preservation may be safe in select premenopausal patients with stage IA G1 endometrioid cancer, normal ovaries, no family history of ovarian cancer or Lynch syndrome, and absence of p53 abnormality, MMR deficiency, LVSI, and positive cytology. Salpingectomy recommended.
For uterine sarcomas, expert pathology review is critical; morcellation contraindicated. Lymphadenectomy controversial and not routinely recommended unless clinical suspicion.
Oophorectomy individualized for reproductive-age patients with uterine sarcomas; favor BSO if ER/PR positive.

procedures.name: Total Hysterectomy (TH) with Bilateral Salpingo-Oophorectomy (BSO)
procedures.approach: Minimally invasive (laparoscopic, robotic) preferred over laparotomy.
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for uterine-confined endometrial carcinoma and most uterine sarcomas.
procedures.key_complications: Surgical site infection, transfusion, venous thromboembolism, urinary or gastrointestinal injury, lymphedema.
procedures.operative_mortality: Low, but increased in older patients with comorbidities.

procedures.name: Radical Hysterectomy (RH)
procedures.indications: Stage II disease with cervical stromal involvement to obtain negative margins.
procedures.key_complications: Increased risk of urinary complications (e.g., bladder atony, ureteral injury) compared to TH.

procedures.name: Sentinel Lymph Node (SLN) Mapping and Biopsy
procedures.approach: Cervical injection with indocyanine green (ICG) preferred; side-specific nodal dissection if mapping fails.
procedures.indications: Apparent uterine-confined endometrial carcinoma for staging.
procedures.key_complications: Lymphedema (reduced compared to full lymphadenectomy), false-negative results.

procedures.name: Surgical Staging/Debulking for Advanced Disease
procedures.approach: Laparotomy or minimally invasive depending on disease extent.
procedures.indications: Suspected extrauterine disease (abdominal/pelvic-confined) with goal of no measurable residual disease.
procedures.key_complications: Similar to TH/BSO plus potential for bowel resection, splenectomy, etc.

procedures.name: Metastasectomy for Oligometastatic Disease
procedures.approach: Individualized based on site (e.g., pulmonary resection).
procedures.indications: Isolated metastases in uterine sarcoma or endometrial carcinoma with controlled primary.
procedures.operative_mortality: Low to moderate depending on procedure.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Disease is amenable to complete surgical resection with acceptable morbidity. Factors include performance status, comorbidities, extent of disease (local vs. metastatic), and patient preference. For uterine sarcomas, resectability is assessed based on symptoms, extent of disease, and possibility of achieving negative margins.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Optimize medical comorbidities (e.g., diabetes, hypertension), nutritional status, and performance status. Consider preoperative embolization for large tumors. For uterine sarcomas, avoid morcellation; consider imaging to assess resectability.

radiation_therapy.role: Used for adjuvant treatment to improve locoregional control, definitive treatment for medically inoperable patients, and palliation for recurrent/metastatic disease.
principles: RT is directed at sites of known or suspected tumor involvement and may include EBRT and/or brachytherapy.
Imaging required to assess locoregional extent and rule out distant metastases before RT.
EBRT typically targets pelvis with or without para-aortic region. Brachytherapy delivered to intact uterus preoperatively/definitively or to vagina post-hysterectomy.
Whole abdominal radiotherapy is not considered tumor-directed RT and is no longer recommended due to toxicity.
Chemoradiation can be given concurrently or sequentially.
For uterine sarcomas, adjuvant RT reduces local recurrence but no consistent OS benefit; role controversial.

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant Pelvic EBRT
dose_frameworks.indication: Microscopic disease in pelvis and para-aortic nodes for high-risk endometrial carcinoma or uterine sarcomas.

dose_frameworks.name: Boost for Gross Residual Disease
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative gross residual disease or gross nodal disease (boost to 60–65 Gy).

dose_frameworks.name: Neoadjuvant Radiation
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45–50 Gy ± HDR brachytherapy boost to total EQD2 75–80 Gy
dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: EBRT: 1.8–2.0 Gy; HDR: 5–7 Gy per fraction
dose_frameworks.indication: To minimize positive margins at hysterectomy for cervical involvement or locally advanced disease.

dose_frameworks.name: Postoperative Vaginal Brachytherapy Alone
dose_frameworks.indication: Adjuvant for low-intermediate risk endometrial carcinoma (e.g., Stage IA G1-2, Stage IB G1).

dose_frameworks.name: Brachytherapy Boost to EBRT
dose_frameworks.indication: Boost to vaginal cuff after hysterectomy for higher risk features.

dose_frameworks.name: Definitive Brachytherapy for Medically Inoperable Uterine Cancer
dose_frameworks.total_dose: EQD2 D90 ≥48 Gy (brachytherapy alone) or ≥65 Gy (combined EBRT + brachytherapy); GTV target ≥80 Gy with MRI guidance.

approaches.name: Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy (IMRT)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Cisplatin-based concurrent chemoradiation for high-risk disease (category 1 for some histologies).
approaches.indication: Pelvic and para-aortic RT for endometrial carcinoma and uterine sarcomas; preferred to minimize toxicity.
approaches.toxicities: Diarrhea, bowel symptoms, fatigue, secondary malignancies (long-term).

approaches.name: High-Dose-Rate (HDR) Brachytherapy
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Can be interdigitated with chemotherapy for carcinosarcoma.
approaches.indication: Adjuvant vaginal brachytherapy, definitive treatment for medically inoperable disease.

approaches.name: Stereotactic Body Radiation Therapy (SBRT)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 1–5 fractions of ablative doses (e.g., 30–50 Gy in 1–5 fractions).
approaches.indication: Oligometastatic disease (1–5 metastatic lesions) if primary cancer controlled.
approaches.toxicities: Site-specific (e.g., pneumonitis for lung, hepatotoxicity for liver).

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy is used in adjuvant, primary, and recurrent/metastatic settings. Regimens include chemotherapy, immunotherapy, targeted therapy, and hormonal therapy. Choice depends on histology, stage, molecular profile (MSI-H/dMMR, HER2, NTRK, RET, TMB, BRCA, CDK4), and prior treatment. Clinical trial enrollment strongly encouraged.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Primary or Adjuvant Therapy (Stage III–IV Endometrial Carcinoma)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab (category 1, except for carcinosarcoma), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Dostarlimab (category 1), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Durvalumab (for dMMR tumors only, category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Improved PFS and OS based on NRG-GY018, RUBY, and DUO-E trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Trastuzumab (for HER2-positive uterine serous carcinoma or carcinosarcoma), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab, Carboplatin/Paclitaxel. For pMMR tumors: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel/Durvalumab + Durvalumab/Olaparib (category 2B).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR status for durvalumab regimen; HER2 testing for trastuzumab regimen; PD-L1 not required for pembrolizumab or dostarlimab in this setting.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Therapy for Recurrent Endometrial Carcinoma
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab (category 1, except for carcinosarcoma), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Dostarlimab (category 1), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Durvalumab (for dMMR tumors only, category 1).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Similar to adjuvant setting; based on NRG-GY018, RUBY, DUO-E trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Trastuzumab (for HER2-positive), Carboplatin/Paclitaxel (category 1 for carcinosarcoma), Carboplatin/Docetaxel, Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab. For pMMR tumors: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (category 1 after prior platinum therapy). Biomarker-directed: Pembrolizumab for TMB-H or MSI-H/dMMR; Dostarlimab for MSI-H/dMMR; Axitinib + Avelumab for pMMR.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR testing recommended if not previously done; TMB-H testing if not previously done; HER2 testing for carcinosarcoma; NTRK, RET gene fusion testing for metastatic/recurrent disease.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy for Recurrent Endometrial Carcinoma
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Not specified; options include single-agent chemotherapy or biomarker-directed therapy.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on prior treatment and biomarkers.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/Doxorubicin, Cisplatin/Doxorubicin/Paclitaxel, Cisplatin/Gemcitabine, single agents (Cisplatin, Carboplatin, Doxorubicin, Liposomal Doxorubicin, Paclitaxel, Albumin-bound Paclitaxel, Topotecan, Bevacizumab, Temsirolimus, Cabozantinib, Lenvatinib, Gemcitabine, Docetaxel [category 2B], Ifosfamide [for carcinosarcoma], Ifosfamide/Paclitaxel [for carcinosarcoma], Cisplatin/Ifosfamide [for carcinosarcoma]). Biomarker-directed: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab (category 1 for pMMR), Axitinib + Avelumab (pMMR), Pembrolizumab (TMB-H or MSI-H/dMMR), Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (TMB-H), Dostarlimab, Avelumab, Nivolumab, Tislelizumab-jsgr, Retifanlimab-dlwr (category 2B for MSI-H/dMMR), Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (HER2-positive, including for carcinosarcoma with HER2 1+/2+/3+), Larotrectinib, Entrectinib, Repotrectinib (NTRK fusion-positive), Selpercatinib (RET fusion-positive).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, HER2, NTRK, RET testing as above.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Hormonal Therapy for Recurrent/Metastatic Endometrial Carcinoma
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For lower-grade endometrioid histologies with ER/PR positivity, small tumor volume or indolent growth.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Medroxyprogesterone acetate/Tamoxifen (alternating), Progestational agents (Medroxyprogesterone acetate, Megestrol acetate), Aromatase inhibitors (Anastrozole, Letrozole, Exemestane), Tamoxifen, Fulvestrant. Useful in certain circumstances for ER-positive tumors: Letrozole/Ribociclib, Abemaciclib/Letrozole, Letrozole/Palbociclib, Abemaciclib/Fulvestrant.
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: ER/PR testing recommended for stage III, IV, and recurrent endometrioid carcinoma.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Hormonal Therapy for Uterine-Limited Disease Not Suitable for Surgery or for Fertility Preservation
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Continuous progestin-based therapy (Megestrol acetate, Medroxyprogesterone acetate), Dual progestin agents (Levonorgestrel IUD/Megestrol acetate, Levonorgestrel IUD/Medroxyprogesterone acetate).

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Advanced, Recurrent/Metastatic or Inoperable Uterine Sarcoma
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: First-line: Doxorubicin, Docetaxel/Gemcitabine, Doxorubicin/Trabectedin (for LMS), Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide, Doxorubicin/Dacarbazine (for LMS or ifosfamide ineligible). Second-line: Trabectedin (for LMS).
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Clinical trials strongly recommended; limited data for most agents.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Second-line: Dacarbazine/Gemcitabine, Gemcitabine/Vinorelbine, Dacarbazine, Gemcitabine, Epirubicin, Ifosfamide, Liposomal Doxorubicin, Pazopanib, Temozolomide, Regorafenib, Eribulin (category 2B). Biomarker-directed: Pembrolizumab (TMB-H), Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (TMB-H), PARP inhibitors for BRCA-altered LMS (Olaparib, Rucaparib, Niraparib), PEComa (Sirolimus, Everolimus, Temsirolimus), CDK4 amplification (Palbociclib).
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI, BRCA 1/2, NTRK 1/2/3, RET, ALK, TMB testing in metastatic disease; PEComa or TSC pathogenic variant for mTOR inhibitors; CDK4 amplification for palbociclib.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Anti-Estrogen Hormone Therapy for Low-Grade ESS or Adenosarcoma Without SO (ER/PR-Positive Uterine Sarcomas)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: For ER/PR-positive tumors, preferably with small tumor volume or indolent growth pace.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Fulvestrant, Megestrol acetate (category 2B), Medroxyprogesterone acetate (category 2B). Consider GnRH analogs with aromatase inhibitors for premenopausal patients not suitable for BSO.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: Primary or adjuvant therapy for stage III–IV endometrial carcinoma (except carcinosarcoma); first-line for recurrent disease.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, anemia, thrombocytopenia, fatigue, nausea, neuropathy, immune-related adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Dostarlimab
key_regimens.setting: Primary or adjuvant therapy for stage III–IV endometrial carcinoma; first-line for recurrent disease.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Similar to carboplatin/paclitaxel plus immune-related adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Durvalumab
key_regimens.setting: Primary or adjuvant therapy for stage III–IV dMMR endometrial carcinoma; first-line for recurrent dMMR disease.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Similar to carboplatin/paclitaxel plus immune-related adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel/Durvalumab + Durvalumab/Olaparib
key_regimens.setting: Primary or adjuvant therapy for stage III–IV pMMR endometrial carcinoma (category 2B); first-line for recurrent pMMR disease.
key_regimens.cycle_days: Induction: 21-day cycle for 6 cycles; maintenance: durvalumab 1120 mg IV every 4 weeks ± olaparib 300 mg BID until progression.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, fatigue, nausea, immune-related adverse events, anemia, thrombocytopenia.

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Trastuzumab
key_regimens.setting: Primary or adjuvant therapy for stage III–IV HER2-positive uterine serous carcinoma or carcinosarcoma; first-line for recurrent HER2-positive disease.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, fatigue, nausea, neuropathy, cardiac toxicity (monitor left ventricular function).

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab
key_regimens.setting: Primary or adjuvant therapy for stage III–IV endometrial carcinoma; first-line for recurrent disease.

key_regimens.name: Lenvatinib + Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: First-line therapy for recurrent endometrial carcinoma (pMMR tumors after prior platinum therapy); second-line or subsequent therapy for pMMR tumors.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, diarrhea, fatigue, hypothyroidism, hepatotoxicity, immune-related adverse events.

key_regimens.name: Doxorubicin
key_regimens.setting: First-line therapy for advanced, recurrent/metastatic or inoperable uterine sarcoma.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea, alopecia, cardiotoxicity (cumulative dose limit).

key_regimens.name: Docetaxel/Gemcitabine
key_regimens.setting: First-line therapy for advanced, recurrent/metastatic or inoperable uterine sarcoma.

key_regimens.name: Doxorubicin/Trabectedin
key_regimens.setting: First-line therapy for metastatic or unresectable leiomyosarcoma (LMS).

key_regimens.name: Trabectedin
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent therapy for metastatic or unresectable leiomyosarcoma (LMS).

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response and Monitoring
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessed during and after primary or adjuvant therapy; surveillance per guidelines.
response_logic: For patients receiving systemic therapy, response assessed using imaging (CT, MRI) and clinical assessment per RECIST criteria.
For fertility-sparing therapy, endometrial evaluation every 3–6 months (hysteroscopy, biopsy, D&C) to assess response; complete response defined as no disease on sampling.
For uterine sarcomas, imaging surveillance to assess for recurrence or metastatic disease.
Molecular response not routinely assessed; clinical correlation essential.
imaging_recommendations: During treatment: imaging as clinically indicated based on symptoms or to assess response to neoadjuvant therapy.
Post-treatment: surveillance imaging per histology and stage (see ENDO-9, UTSARC-4).
For suspected recurrence: CT abdomen/pelvis and/or chest CT based on symptoms; consider FDG-PET/CT or MRI in select patients.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: For suspected recurrence, biopsy may confirm histology and guide therapy.
For locoregional recurrence, salvage options include surgery, RT, or systemic therapy based on prior treatment and resectability.
For distant metastases, biopsy may be performed to confirm diagnosis or for molecular testing (e.g., HER2, NTRK, RET).
For persistent disease on fertility-sparing therapy, salvage includes TH/BSO with staging.

prognosis.overall: The prognosis for uterine neoplasms is highly variable depending on histology, stage, grade, and molecular profile. Endometrial carcinoma is the fourth most common female genital tract malignancy in the US, with an estimated 69,120 new cases and 13,860 deaths in 2025 [1]. Uterine sarcomas are uncommon, accounting for about 3% of uterine cancers [2,3]. Mortality rates for uterine cancer have increased more rapidly than incidence, possibly due to rising rates of advanced-stage disease and high-risk histologies [4].

by_stage.context: SEER data; survival is increased in patients who are younger, have early-stage disease, and have lower-grade disease [5].
Nodal involvement is a major adverse prognostic factor [6].
Distant disease has a poor prognosis; 5-year survival is significantly reduced [5].

prognostic_factors: Age (older age is a poor prognostic factor) [4,5]
Histologic grade (higher grade indicates worse prognosis) [4]
Myometrial invasion depth (deeper invasion is associated with higher risk) [7]
Lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI), especially substantial LVSI [6,7]
Histologic subtype (e.g., serous, clear cell, carcinosarcoma, undifferentiated) [8]
Molecular profile (POLE-mutated has excellent prognosis; p53-aberrant has poor prognosis) [9,10]
Presence of peritoneal cytology (its independent prognostic value is questioned) [11]
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis is determined by a combination of clinical, pathologic, and molecular factors. Thorough surgical staging provides critical prognostic information that guides adjuvant therapy. Molecular classification into POLE-mutated, dMMR/MSI-H, NSMP, and p53-aberrant subgroups offers significant prognostic and predictive insight [9,10]. Patients with POLE-mutated tumors have the best prognosis, while p53-aberrant tumors are the most aggressive.

disease_related.management: Evaluation with endometrial biopsy or D&C; may be the initial presenting symptom leading to diagnosis.
Imaging and clinical evaluation; may indicate advanced or recurrent disease.

disease_related.complication: Metastatic disease (e.g., to lung, liver, bone, peritoneum)
disease_related.management: Systemic therapy, local therapy (surgery, radiation) for isolated metastases, or palliative care.

chemotherapy: Infusion reactions (more common with taxanes and platinum agents) [13]
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) such as colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies, dermatologic reactions [14]
surgery: Bowel or urinary complications (e.g., obstruction, fistula) [18]

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral to managing patients with uterine neoplasms, addressing both disease-related and treatment-related toxicities. It includes symptom management, psychosocial support, and survivorship care [18].
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional assessment and counseling are recommended to manage weight changes, especially in patients with obesity, a risk factor for endometrial cancer. Weight management and lifestyle modification are encouraged [18].
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Antiemetic prophylaxis based on the emetogenic potential of chemotherapy regimens (e.g., high emetogenic risk for cisplatin, moderate for carboplatin/paclitaxel) [12]. Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) support may be considered for chemotherapy regimens with a high risk of febrile neutropenia (e.g., >20%) [12].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) prophylaxis is recommended for patients undergoing major pelvic surgery and may be considered for those receiving chemotherapy, especially with bevacizumab [15,17].
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain assessment and management using WHO analgesic ladder; may include NSAIDs, opioids, and adjuvant agents. Referral to pain specialist as needed.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Psychosocial effects may include depression, anxiety, fear of recurrence, financial concerns, and issues with sexuality and intimacy. Referral to psychotherapy, support groups, and financial counseling is recommended [18].
supportive_care.dental_care: Dental evaluation prior to radiation therapy to the pelvis; management of oral complications from chemotherapy (mucositis, xerostomia).

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After completing primary treatment, patients enter a surveillance phase. The goal is to detect recurrence early when curative salvage therapy may be possible. Surveillance includes regular history and physical exam, symptom evaluation, and selected imaging [20].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Intensive surveillance has not been shown to improve overall survival in endometrial cancer, but it provides psychosocial reassurance and allows for early detection of recurrences, which are often symptomatic [20]. Most recurrences occur within the first 3 years after treatment.
late_effects_screening: Screening for osteoporosis, especially after bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (BSO) or pelvic radiation [18]
Assessment for sexual dysfunction, vaginal stenosis, and vaginal dryness [18]
Monitoring for secondary malignancies related to prior radiation [19]
Assessment of cardiovascular risk factors and metabolic syndrome, particularly in patients with obesity [18]
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most recurrences of endometrial cancer are vaginal or pelvic (locoregional), but distant metastases (e.g., to lung, abdomen) also occur. For uterine sarcomas, especially leiomyosarcoma, hematogenous spread to the lungs is common [21].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: Physical exam (including pelvic exam) every 3-6 months for 2-3 years, then every 6-12 months for up to year 5, then annually [20].
For uterine sarcoma: Consider H&P every 3-4 months for 2-3 years, then every 6-12 months [21].
Symptom assessment for potential recurrence (bleeding, pain, weight loss, respiratory symptoms) [20].
imaging_strategy: Imaging should be guided by symptoms and clinical suspicion, not performed routinely in asymptomatic patients [20].
For endometrial cancer: Consider chest imaging if symptoms or high-risk features; imaging as indicated for suspected recurrence [22].
For uterine sarcoma: Chest/abdomen/pelvis CT every 3-6 months for 3 years, then every 6-12 months for 2 years; optional imaging beyond 5 years depending on histology and stage [21].
FDG-PET/CT may be considered to clarify ambiguous findings [22].
laboratory_monitoring: CA-125 if initially elevated or for serous histology to monitor response [20].
Routine tumor markers are not recommended for all patients [20].
supportive_follow_up: Health maintenance: blood pressure, breast exam, mammography, stool guaiac, immunizations as appropriate [18].
Counseling on lifestyle: exercise, smoking cessation, nutrition, and weight management [18].
Assessment of late effects of treatment and referral to specialists (e.g., pelvic floor therapy, sexual health, psychotherapy) [18].
Survivorship care plan summarizing treatment and follow-up recommendations [18].

special_situations.key_message: Continuous progestin-based therapy may be considered for highly selected patients with grade 1, stage IA (noninvasive) endometrioid adenocarcinoma who desire uterine preservation.
special_situations.management: Levonorgestrel IUD (preferred) or oral progestins (megestrol acetate, medroxyprogesterone). Endometrial sampling every 3-6 months. If complete response, encourage conception. Consider hysterectomy after childbearing is complete or if disease persists/progresses [23].
special_situations.evidence: Based on observational studies showing high response rates but also high recurrence rates. Must meet strict criteria: G1 endometrioid, disease limited to endometrium, no myoinvasion on MRI, no contraindications to progestins [23].

special_situations.key_message: Treatment depends on the site of recurrence, prior therapy, histology, and molecular profile.
special_situations.management: Locoregional recurrence: Consider resection and/or radiation. Disseminated metastases: Systemic therapy (chemotherapy, immunotherapy, hormonal therapy) ± palliative radiation. Biomarker-directed therapy for specific molecular alterations [24].
special_situations.evidence: Pembrolizumab + lenvatinib for pMMR tumors (category 1) [25]. Pembrolizumab or dostarlimab for dMMR/MSI-H tumors [26,27].

special_situations.situation: High-risk histologies (serous, clear cell, carcinosarcoma, undifferentiated)
special_situations.key_message: These aggressive subtypes require multimodality therapy even for early-stage disease.
special_situations.management: Primary treatment: TH/BSO with surgical staging, maximal debulking for gross disease. Adjuvant systemic therapy ± radiation recommended for most stages. HER2 testing for serous and carcinosarcoma [8].
special_situations.evidence: Carboplatin/paclitaxel + trastuzumab for HER2-positive serous carcinoma or carcinosarcoma (category 1) [28].

special_situations.key_message: Uterine sarcomas (e.g., leiomyosarcoma, endometrial stromal sarcoma) are managed differently from endometrial carcinoma.
special_situations.management: Surgery with en bloc resection (no morcellation) is primary. BSO favored for ER/PR-positive tumors. Lymphadenectomy is controversial. Systemic therapy for advanced disease: doxorubicin, gemcitabine/docetaxel, trabectedin (for LMS) [21].
special_situations.evidence: Doxorubicin/trabectedin improved PFS vs. doxorubicin alone in LMS (LMS-04 trial) [29].

special_situations.key_message: Endometrial cancer can be the first manifestation of Lynch syndrome. Genetic testing and counseling are essential.
special_situations.management: Germline testing for MMR genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) or EPCAM. Manage accordingly: surveillance for other cancers, risk-reducing surgery [30].
special_situations.evidence: Patients with Lynch syndrome have a 60% lifetime risk of endometrial cancer; relatives may also be at risk [30].

clinical_pearls: Molecular classification (POLE-mutated, dMMR, NSMP, p53-aberrant) is now central to prognosis and may guide therapy in endometrial cancer [9,10].

Ovarian preservation may be safe in select premenopausal patients with stage IA G1 endometrioid cancer, normal-appearing ovaries, no family history of ovarian cancer or Lynch syndrome, and absence of p53 abnormality, dMMR, LVSI, and positive cytology [31].
Sentinel lymph node (SLN) mapping with ultrastaging is the preferred alternative to full lymphadenectomy for staging apparent uterine-confined endometrial cancer [32].
Minimally invasive surgery (laparoscopy or robotic) is preferred for staging when feasible due to lower perioperative complications without compromising oncologic outcomes [17].
Carcinosarcoma is treated as a high-grade endometrial carcinoma, not a sarcoma [8].
In recurrent endometrial cancer, lenvatinib/pembrolizumab is a category 1 preferred option for pMMR tumors after prior platinum-based therapy [25].
For dMMR/MSI-H recurrent endometrial cancer, pembrolizumab or dostarlimab are highly effective and preferred [26,27].
Morcellation should be avoided when sarcoma is suspected, as it can lead to peritoneal spread [21].

guidelines_resources.notes: Version 3.2026; updated June 16, 2026. Provides comprehensive management recommendations for endometrial carcinoma and uterine sarcoma [33].

guidelines_resources.notes: Provides guidelines for follow-up after treatment of gynecologic cancers [20].

guidelines_resources.title: ESMO-ESGO-ESTRO Consensus Conference on Endometrial Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides evidence-based recommendations for diagnosis, treatment, and follow-up of endometrial cancer [34].

guidelines_resources.organization: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG)
guidelines_resources.title: ACOG Practice Bulletin: Management of Endometrial Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides guidance on diagnosis and management of endometrial cancer [35].

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab plus Chemotherapy in Advanced Endometrial Cancer
key_trials.population: Stage III or IVA endometrial carcinoma with measurable disease, or stage IVB or recurrent disease, any histologic subtype except carcinosarcoma.
key_trials.key_result: PFS was 74% vs. 38% in the dMMR cohort (HR, 0.30; P<.001). In pMMR tumors, median PFS was 13.1 vs. 8.7 months (HR, 0.54; P<.001) [26].
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab + carboplatin/paclitaxel as a preferred first-line regimen for advanced/recurrent endometrial cancer, except carcinosarcoma.

key_trials.full_name: Dostarlimab for Primary Advanced or Recurrent Endometrial Cancer
key_trials.population: Stage III or IV or recurrent endometrial cancer, all histologies.
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: OS benefit in the overall population and in dMMR/MSI-H subgroup.
key_trials.practice_change: Established dostarlimab + carboplatin/paclitaxel as a preferred first-line regimen for advanced/recurrent endometrial cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Durvalumab Plus Carboplatin/Paclitaxel with or without Olaparib in Advanced Endometrial Cancer
key_trials.key_result: PFS benefit for durvalumab (HR, 0.71; P=.003) and durvalumab+olaparib (HR, 0.55; P<.0001) vs. control. Greatest benefit in dMMR subgroup [14].
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS in pMMR subgroup: HR 0.57 for durvalumab+olaparib vs. control.
key_trials.practice_change: Carboplatin/paclitaxel + durvalumab is a preferred first-line regimen for dMMR tumors only.

key_trials.full_name: Adjuvant Chemoradiotherapy versus Radiotherapy Alone for High-Risk Endometrial Cancer
key_trials.population: High-risk endometrial cancer (stage I grade 3 with deep invasion/LVSI, stage II, stage III, or any serous/clear cell).
key_trials.practice_change: Established adjuvant chemoradiation as standard for high-risk endometrial cancer.

key_trials.full_name: Lenvatinib plus Pembrolizumab versus Chemotherapy in Advanced Endometrial Cancer
key_trials.population: pMMR (MSI-stable) advanced endometrial cancer after prior systemic therapy.
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: 7.2 vs. 3.8 months (HR, 0.56; P<.001). Median OS: 18.3 vs. 11.4 months (HR, 0.62; P<.001) [25].
key_trials.practice_change: Established lenvatinib/pembrolizumab as a preferred second-line option for pMMR tumors.

key_trials.full_name: Doxorubicin alone versus Doxorubicin with Trabectedin as First-Line Therapy for Metastatic or Unresectable Leiomyosarcoma
key_trials.population: Metastatic or unresectable leiomyosarcoma (soft tissue or uterine).
key_trials.practice_change: Established doxorubicin/trabectedin as a preferred first-line regimen for advanced LMS.
